rationale: This is the classical and most frequently cited example of the Ritter reaction, involving the reaction of tert-butanol with acetonitrile under strong acid catalysis (sulfuric acid). Tert-butanol readily forms a stable tert-butyl carbocation, which is then trapped by the nitrile. Subsequent hydrolysis of the nitrilium ion intermediate yields N-tert-butylacetamide. All reactants and products are simple, acyclic, and lack complex functional groups, making it a clear and unambiguous representation of the core transformation.
collision_avoidance_note: This example uses simple, acyclic aliphatic substrates (tert-butanol and acetonitrile) to avoid overlap with reactions involving aromatic rings (e.g., Friedel-Crafts), complex rearrangements, or other named reactions that might also use strong acids. The core transformation is clearly the formation of a C-N bond from a carbocation source and a nitrile, followed by hydrolysis to an amide, which is the defining feature of the Ritter reaction.
